planting.note: Plant root divisions in spring or fall in any reasonably moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; creeping comfrey spreads vigorously by underground stolons in favorable conditions but is far less invasive than common comfrey. The smallest piece of root will grow into a new plant.

directions: Plant in moist to average, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; creeping comfrey is the most refined and garden-worthy of the comfreys for shade groundcover use, forming a low, dense mat of hairy, elliptical leaves with pale cream to yellow tubular flowers in spring that attract bumblebees. Unlike the vigorous and sometimes invasive S. x uplandicum (Russian comfrey) and S. officinale, creeping comfrey stays low to 12-14 inches and spreads at a moderate pace rather than becoming aggressively weedy. The leaves are mineral-rich and can be cut repeatedly and used as mulch or compost accelerator, as with other comfreys, but this species is primarily grown as an ornamental shade groundcover. Excellent for suppressing weeds under trees and shrubs where little else grows. The semi-evergreen foliage provides year-round weed suppression in mild winters.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost in well-drained, humus-rich starting mix; transplant to a shaded position with trellis support after last frost. The plant needs 3-5 years from seed to produce roots large enough for medicinal harvest.

directions: Plant in moist, deep, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade with a trellis or fence to climb; dang shen is one of the most important tonic herbs in Traditional Chinese Medicine, used as an energy-building adaptogen and substitute for the more expensive Panax ginseng. The twining, climbing vine produces pale greenish-yellow bell-shaped flowers with purple-veined interiors in summer. The thick, fleshy taproot is harvested after 3-5 years and used in soups, teas, and tonic preparations throughout China, Korea, and Japan. Modern research confirms adaptogenic compounds in the root that support energy and immune function. The plant dies back in winter and re-emerges from the root in spring. Grow in a position where the deep taproot will not be disturbed.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, sandy or rocky, acidic soil in full sun; lithodora is intolerant of alkaline soils and poorly drained conditions. Root or stem cuttings taken in early summer are the most reliable propagation method.

directions: Plant in well-drained, acidic, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; lithodora is one of the most intensely blue-flowered plants in horticulture, with flowers of a saturated, true azure-blue that stops visitors in their tracks when the plant is in full bloom in spring and early summer. It forms a low, spreading, woody-based mat of small, dark green, evergreen leaves covered in tiny blue trumpets. Formerly classified as Lithospermum diffusum. The cultivar 'Grace Ward' has particularly large, deep blue flowers; 'Heavenly Blue' is the most commonly cultivated. Strictly requires acidic, free-draining soil — it collapses rapidly in alkaline or heavy soil. Excellent in rock gardens, along walls, and cascading over raised bed edges. Best in areas with cool, humid summers (Pacific Northwest, coastal areas) and struggles in hot, humid inland climates.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds on the surface of moist soil in partial shade after last frost; do not cover seeds with soil as they require light to germinate. Indian tobacco self-sows freely once established in a woodland garden.

directions: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface (do not cover) in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade after last frost; Indian tobacco is a native annual wildflower of eastern North American woodland clearings and disturbed areas, recognized by its inflated, balloon-like seed capsules after the small, pale lavender-blue flowers fade. It is an important plant in the history of American botanical medicine — the leaves and seeds contain lobeline and related piperidine alkaloids that act as respiratory stimulants and were traditionally used by Indigenous peoples and 19th-century physicians for asthma, croup, and as an emetic. IMPORTANT CAUTION: All parts of the plant are toxic in quantity; it should be used medicinally only under professional guidance. Do not confuse with garden lobelia cultivars (L. erinus) which are ornamentals. Indian tobacco is primarily of botanical and ethnobotanical interest rather than general garden use.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; or cold-stratify seeds 90 days and sow indoors in late winter. Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil at the edge of a stream or in a moist woodland.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade at stream banks, moist woodland edges, and wet mountain meadows; tall bluebells is the western counterpart of Virginia bluebells (M. virginica), native to streamside habitats from the Rocky Mountains through the Great Basin. The hanging clusters of blue tubular flowers in late spring are typical of the genus — opening from pink buds and maturing to clear sky-blue — and are heavily visited by bumblebees and hummingbirds. The plant goes fully dormant by midsummer after setting seed, so mark its location to avoid digging through the dormant rootstock. Combines beautifully with marsh marigolds, skunk cabbage, and riparian sedges in moist, shaded stream-bank planting. Best in cool-summer climates.
